coredata.dc:title: Rapid and dual-mode nitrite detection with improved sensitivity by nanointerface-regulated ultrafast Griess assay
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Jingbo
Long, Tiantian
Chen, Zihan
Luo, Hongmei
Cao, Jiafeng
Xu, Dong
Yuan, Zhiqin
coredata.dc:description:
Background The rapid and sensitive detection of nitrite is important to human health protection due to its carcinogenic and teratogenic risks with excessive intake. The Griess assay is widely applied for the design of nitrite detection system. However, its relatively slow reaction kinetics and sole colorimetry mode might limit it's the sensitivity and practical application. Therefore, it is highly desirable to explore new detection method with rapid kinetics and multi-mode recognition characters.
Results We report a rapid and colorimetry and fluorimetry dual-mode sensing of nitrite by using N-(1-naphthalene)-ethylenediamine-derivated carbon dots (NETH-CDs) as the reporters. NETH-CDs show maximum excitation and emission around 359 nm and 431 nm, accompanying with a quantum yield of 27.1 %. The carbonization of NETH not only increases the fluorescence sensing mode, but also promotes the nanointerfacial Griess assay reaction kinetics within 1 min. High selectivity of dual-mode sensing is exhibited due to the specific reaction. The linear ranges in colorimetry and fluorimetry modes are 0.2–100 μM, and the corresponding limit of detection values are determined to be 0.10 and 0.08 μM, respectively. In addition, the accurate nitrite analysis in urine and serum samples with NETH-CDs nanoprobes. Moreover, the visual nitrite detection is achieved based on NETH-CD-loaded test strips.
Significance This work reports a new dual mode nitrite detection method for the first time by NETH-CDs supported ultrafast interfacial Griess assay, it not only explores sensitive nitrite detection probe, but also provides deep understanding of the relationship between chemical reaction kinetics and interfacial interaction on nanosurface. We believe our findings would benefit the exploration of rapid and sensitive detection systems toward various targets by regulating interfacial chemistry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanointerface
Ultrafast reaction
Nitrite detection
Dual-mode sensing
Griess assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Visual nitrite sensing by point-of-care testing
Sensitivity and selectivity of dual-mode nitrite sensing
Nitrite analysis in biological and food samples
NETH-CDs-based test strips for visual nitrite detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of NETH-CDs supported ultrafast Griess assay for rapid and dual-mode nitrite detection.

Characterization of NETH-CDs. (a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of NETH-CDs. Inserts are photographs of NETH-CDs solution under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV (left) light. (b) FT-IR spectra of NETH (blue line) and NETH-CDs (green line). (c) Time-resolved fluorescence spectra of NETH-CDs. (d) Raman spectra of NETH-CDs. (e) HR-TEM images and corresponding particle size distribution statistics (inset image). (f) The total XPS spectra of NETH-CDs. (g–i) The XPS spectra of C1s (g), N1s (h), and O1s (i), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and Fluorescence emission spectra (b) of NETH-CDs solution in the absence and presence of sole 100 μM NaNO2, 23.8 mM HCl, 0.9 mM Sa and the mixture of them.

The optimization of experimental conditions. The absorbance increment at 538 nm (a) and relative fluorescence decrement at 431 nm (b) of NETH-CDs solution versus the Sa concentration from 0.45 to 5.4 mM in the presence of nitrite and HCl. The absorbance increment at 538 nm (c) and relative fluorescence decrement at 431 nm (d) of NETH-CDs solution versus the HCl concentration from 47.7 to 166.7 mM in the presence of nitrite and Sa. Time-dependent absorbance increment at 538 nm (e) and relative fluorescence decrement at 431 nm (f) of NETH-CDs solution in the presence of Sa, HCl, and nitrite.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of NETH-CDs solution after the addtion of NaNO2 with various concentrations. (b) Plots of ΔA at 538 nm versus the concentration of NaNO2 over the range of 0.2–100 μM. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of NETH-CDs solution after the addtion of NaNO2 with various concentrations. (d) Plots of (F0– F)/F0 at 431 nm versus the concentration of NaNO2 over the range of 0.2–100 μM.
The selectivity over other anions such as SO4 −, CO3 2−, CH3COO−, S2 −, Cl−, PO4 3−, I−, NO3 −, and Br− under colorimetry (a) and fluorescence (b) mode.

(a) Schematic illustration of visual nitrite detection with NETH-CDs loaded test strips. (b) The photographs of test strips with the addition of nitrite in the concentration of 0–300 μM under natural light (upper) and UV light (bottom) illumination. The extracted data from red channel (c, colorimetry) and blue channel (d, fluorimetry) of test strips with the addition of nitrite. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Comparison of the present method with some reported assays for NaNO2 determination.
Nitrite analysis in biological and food samples with NETH-CDs nanoprobes (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Rapid and dual-mode nitrite detection with improved sensitivity by nanointerface-regulated ultrafast Griess assay

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Laboratory for Rice and By-products Further Processing, College of Food Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry & Technology, Changsha, 410004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Laboratory for Rice and By-products Further Processing
College of Food Science and Engineering
Central South University of Forestry & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Laboratory for Rice and By-products Further Processing, College of Food Science and Engineering, Central South University of Forestry & Technology, Changsha, 410004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing, 100029, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid and sensitive detection of nitrite is important to human health protection due to its carcinogenic and teratogenic risks with excessive intake. The Griess assay is widely applied for the design of nitrite detection system. However, its relatively slow reaction kinetics and sole colorimetry mode might limit it's the sensitivity and practical application. Therefore, it is highly desirable to explore new detection method with rapid kinetics and multi-mode recognition characters.

We report a rapid and colorimetry and fluorimetry dual-mode sensing of nitrite by using N-(1-naphthalene)-ethylenediamine-derivated carbon dots (NETH-CDs) as the reporters. NETH-CDs show maximum excitation and emission around 359 nm and 431 nm, accompanying with a quantum yield of 27.1 %. The carbonization of NETH not only increases the fluorescence sensing mode, but also promotes the nanointerfacial Griess assay reaction kinetics within 1 min. High selectivity of dual-mode sensing is exhibited due to the specific reaction. The linear ranges in colorimetry and fluorimetry modes are 0.2–100 μM, and the corresponding limit of detection values are determined to be 0.10 and 0.08 μM, respectively. In addition, the accurate nitrite analysis in urine and serum samples with NETH-CDs nanoprobes. Moreover, the visual nitrite detection is achieved based on NETH-CD-loaded test strips.

This work reports a new dual mode nitrite detection method for the first time by NETH-CDs supported ultrafast interfacial Griess assay, it not only explores sensitive nitrite detection probe, but also provides deep understanding of the relationship between chemical reaction kinetics and interfacial interaction on nanosurface. We believe our findings would benefit the exploration of rapid and sensitive detection systems toward various targets by regulating interfacial chemistry.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the valuable chemicals, nitrite has been widely used in various fields, including commercial manufacture, pharmaceutics, fertilizer industry, and meat processing [1,2]. It is also a part of nitrogen cycle, which widely exists in ground and surface water systems, and even biological system [3]. For example, nitrite could be originated from the oxidation of NO and nitrate reductase-involved bacteria [4,5]. As an important biochemical indicator, nitrite content in body fluids (plasma, serum, and urine) can reflect human physiological health level, being important for disease diagnosis, mediate signal transduction, cellular respiration, innate immunity and treatment [6,7]. It is reported that the excessive intake of nitrite might cause carcinogenic and teratogenic risks to human [8–10]. European Union has set the allowable nitrite limit of 150 ppm in meat products, while the United States permits 100 ppm in most products. The accepted daily NaNO2 intake (ADI) should not exceed 0.1 mg/kg [11,12]. Therefore, the development of sensitive and selective nitrite detection methods is significant to reveal nitrite distribution and protect human health.

Toward this goal, lots of approaches, such as UV–Vis spectroscopy [13], ion chromatography [14], capillary electrophoresis [15], chemiluminescence [16] and electrochemistry [17] are widely developed for analyzing nitrite. In view of the specific chemical reactivity, there are two major chemical reactions for achieving selective nitrite perception, peroxynitrite system and Griess assay. For peroxynitrite system, the nitrite reacts with hydrogen peroxide and forms peroxynitrite, which generates strongly oxidative hydroxyl radical and superoxide anion [18]. These highly reactive oxygen species usually quench the fluorescence and lead to fluorometric variation [19]. In contrast, Griess assay associated with diazotization reaction, is extensively applied for the exploration of nitrite sensing systems due to its mild reaction condition and easy operation [20]. Generally, Griess assay is consist of two steps: nitrite reacts with p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid (Sa) to form a diazonium salt [21], diazonium salt reacts with N-(1-naphthyl)ethylenediamine to form a pink azo dye. Taking this character into consideration, the colorimetric variation has been utilized for nitrite sensing in past decades [22]. The conventional Griess assay requires long time (above 15～30 min) to complete the reaction, hindering the rapid and on-site nitrite perception. Thus, it is appealing to develop rapid and sensitive method toward nitrite detection.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs), a type of zero-dimensional fluorescent quasi spherical carbon particles, have sparked considerable interests in the fields of biological imaging, chemical sensing, and medical applications due to their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, high water solubility, and unique photoluminescent properties [23–26]. Through hydrothermal methods, microwave methods and electrochemical approaches, CDs with tunable emissive characters and surface properties are readily prepared [27,28]. It is reported that interfacial reaction is much faster than homogeneous reaction because of the altered electric field effect and electron transport induced low reaction energy barrier [29]. The colloid CDs with totally immersive nanosurface might provide effective reaction interface. In addition, the reactant can be fixed onto the CD surface via carbonization strategy [30,31]. Therefore, the exploitation of rapid nitrite sensing systems by engineering CD-mediated nanointerface is theoretical possible.

Despite of the report of various CD-based sensing systems, they usually reply on single variety of fluorescence intensity, which might be influenced by the matrix effect. In this work, we proposed a rapid and dual-mode Griess assay for colorimetric and fluorometric nitrite detection by using N-(1-naphthalene)-ethylenediamine-derived CDs (NETH-CDs) as the reporters. Blue emissive NETH-CDs were first prepared through the electrolysis of NETH in PBS solution with 10 V voltage. The NETH-CDs have superior optical properties with the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths located at 359 nm and 431 nm and the quantum yield of 27.1 %. With the introduction of Sa, the nanointerfacial Griess assay could be finished within 1 min, accompanying with both color change and fluorescence quenching (Fig. 1 ). Few parameters that affect sensing performances including Sa concentration, HCl content and reaction time were investigated. Under the optimal condition, highly selective and sensitive (down to 0.10 μM) nitrite detection was realized. Practicality of this system was further validated by accurate nitrite detection in urine and serum samples with NETH-CDs nanoprobes. Moreover, the visual nitrite sensing was conducted with NETH-CD-based test strips. This work not only presents a dual-mode nitrite sensing platform, but also demonstrates the feasibility of enhanced chemical reaction on nanointerface by carbonization strategy.

N-(1-naphthalene)-ethylenediamine dihydrochloride (NETH), p-aminobenzene sulfonic acid (Sa), phosphate buffered saline (PBS, 0.2 M, pH = 7.4), and silver nitrate were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (Shanghai, China). Ammonium sulfate, sodium carbonate, magnesium chloride, potassium bromide, hydrochloric acid, nitrite, and potassium hydroxide were purchased from HUSHI biotech. Inc. (Shanghai, China). Magnesium phosphate and zinc acetate were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The ultra-pure water is produced with a Milipore water purification system (MilI-Q, MilliporeSigma, Burlington, MA, USA).

NETH-CDs were prepared through an electrochemical synthetic method according to our previous work [32]. Electrolysis device was composed with a 25 mL glass container, two platinum electrodes with a length of 4 cm fixed on the cap and a voltage transformer (220 V–10V). NETH as the carbon precursor was dissolved in 0.1 M PBS solution with a final concentration of 1.54 mM. After 15 min eletrolysis, solution color gradually changed from colorless to dark yellow, indicating the formation of NETH-CDs. Then, the formed NETH-CDs were purified by dialysis bag (MW = 500) for 12 h to remove PBS and unreacted NETH prior to further application.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured using a UV-2700 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). The fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were obtained with a G9800A fluorescence spectrophotometer from Agilent (Santa Clara, CA, USA). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were captured using a Titan G2 60–300 microscope (FEI, USA). For HRTEM characterization, 10 μL of the NETH-CDs solution was dropped onto a copper grid and dried before analysis. The FT-IR spectra were recorded using an IR Tracer-100 spectrometer with KBr pellets from Shimadzu (Kyoto, Japan). Raman spectra were obtained with a LabRAM HR Evolution system (HORIBA Scientific, Palaiseau, France) using a 359 nm excitation wavelength. The purified NETH-CDs solution was freeze-dried into powder and used for FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy. The Zeta potential was determined using a Nano-ZS90 analyzer (Malvern, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted with an ESCALAB Xi + spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA). For XPS analysis, 0.05 g of NETH-CDs powder was dissolved in ultrapure water, dropped onto the surface of a silicon wafer and dried at room temperature. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a Panalytical X'Pert PRO instrument (Panalytical, Almelo, Netherlands). The fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were measured using an FLS1000 spectrometer from Edinburgh Instruments (Livingston, England, UK). The NETH-CDs solution was lyophilized at 48 °C and 150 mm Hg for 72 h, resulting in a powder form (Kansas City, MO, USA).

The average fluorescence lifetime (τ ave) was determined as follows:
(1)
τ ave
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
+
B 3
τ 3 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3
Where τ 1, τ 2, and τ 3 represent the fitted lifetime components and B1, B2, B3 stand for the corresponding integrated areas. The excitation and emission wavelengths were set to be 359 nm and 431 nm, respectively.

The fluorescence quenching mechanism of NETH-CDs was analyzed by Stern-Volmer formula [33]:
(2)
F 0
/ F − 1 =
K
S V
[ Q ]
=
K q
τ 0
[ Q ]
Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of NETH-CDs with or without the presence of nitrite, K sv is Stern-Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of nitrite, K q is the quenching rate constant, and τ 0 is the fluorescent lifetime of NETH-CDs.

For feasibility test, to 2.5 mL NETH-CDs solution, 0.12 mL HCl (final concentration: 23.8 mM), 0.12 mL Sa (final concentration: 0.9 mM), and 0.4 mL nitrite solution (final concentration: 100 μM) were added either separately or together. Following a 5-min incubation, the UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of these solutions were measured. For nitrite sensing, 2.5 mL of NETH-CDs solution was mixed with 0.12 mL Sa, 0.12 mL HCl, and 0.4 mL nitrite solution (final concentration: 0, 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80, 90, 100, 150, 200, 300 μM) were added. After 1 min reaction, the corresponding UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were measured. For selectivity evaluation, 100 μM of other anions (SO4 2−, CO3 2−, CH3COO−, S2−, Cl−, PO4 3−, I−, NO3 −, Br-) were used to replaced 60 μM NO2 −.

Blood samples were collected from the volunteers in our lab. The blood was centrifuged immediately at 2500 rpm for 15 min and the supernatant, serum, were collected for further detection. Urine samples were collected from 3 volunteers, and 5 mL urine was filtered with filter paper and collected in test tubes. The samples were stored at 4 °C before use. Spiked samples were made by adding nitrite with the final concentration of 5.0, 8.0, and 16.0 μM. Pickled vegetable and cured meat samples were obtained from a local market. In brief, 5–10 g of pickled vegetable or cured meat were weighted and homogenized using a blender. Then, 50 mL of distilled water was added, followed by shaking for 30 min. After that, the samples were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min. For real sample analysis, 0.4 mL samples were mixed with 0.12 mL Sa and HCl mixture (in UV and fluorescence sensors used 47.7 mM and 166.7 mM HCl respectively to dissolve Sa is 1.8 mM), then mixed 2.5 mL NETH-CDs solution. All the tests were repeated at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual nitrite sensing by point-of-care testing
ce:section.ce:para: Cut Whatman quantitative filter paper into 0.6 cm diameter circular test papers. Immerse these evenly cut strips in NETH-CDs solution for 1 min, then dry them in an oven at 45 °C for 2 h. After drying, store the test papers in a dry and dark environment until further use.

NETH-CDs were synthesized by the electrolysis of 0.2 M PBS diluted NETH solution with a 10 V voltage. After 15 min electrolysis, the solution color changed from colorless to pale yellow. As shown in Fig. 2 a the characteristic absorption of NETH around 330 nm was observed, indicating the existence of NETM on CD surface. These NETH-CDs emit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light, with maxima excitation wavelength at 359 nm and an emission peak at 431 nm. The FT-IR spectrum of NETH-CDs (Fig. 2b) provides insights into molecular vibrations and rotations. The broad absorption peak at 3235 cm⁻1 indicated the presence of O–H and N–H bonds, and it became broader after the formation of NETH-CDs. The absorption peak at 1458 cm⁻1 was assigned to the in-plane C–H bending vibration of the aromatic ring in NETH, which nearly disappeared after carbonization. The characteristic C–OH signal appeared at 1162 cm⁻1 after carbonization, probably due to the oxidization during electrolysis. Additionally, a peak at 1082 cm⁻1 belongs to C–O and C–N stretching vibrations was observed, and CC vibration around 1682 cm⁻1 was also observed. Their relative intensities changed also because of carbonization. These findings confirm the presence of O–H, N–H, –CN–, and C–O–C bonds in the NETH-CDs [34,35]. The fluorescence lift time of the NETH-CDs is 11.01 ns (Fig. 2c). The aqueous synthesis process renders the CDs highly soluble in water, and their quantum yield is measured at 27.1 %. However, lattice fringes could not be observed due to rapid contrast reduction when the electron beam struck the samples. Raman spectroscopy indicates two distinct Raman bands at 1373 cm⁻1 (D band) and 1610 cm⁻1 (G band) for the synthetic NETH-CDs (Fig. 2d). The morphology and size of the CDs were examined using HR-TEM, which revealed quasi-spherical particles with an average diameter of 2.59 nm (Fig. 2e). The D band corresponds to vibrations of defective sp2 carbon regions, while the G band corresponds to vibrations of intact sp2 carbon regions. An (ID/IG) intensity ratio of 0.85 suggests a high degree of graphitization and few defective carbon structures [36,37].

The properties of NETH-CDs are primarily influenced by their surface functional groups. To gain a deeper understanding of the chemical composition of NETH-CDs, XPS analysis was performed (Fig. 2f). The binding energy peaks for C 1s, N 1s, Na KL1, and O 1s were identified. The Na elements present in the NETH-CDs were introduced during electrolysis from the PBS solution. The contents of C, N, and O in the NETH-CDs were calculated to be 34.0 %, 5.4 %, and 60.6 %, respectively. The C 1s detailed XPS spectrum (Fig. 2g) showed that the primary characteristic peak at 284.8 eV is attributed to the strong C–C/CC bonding, which indicates the main features of carbonization [38], making up 35.5 % of the total composition. The peak at 284.8 eV is derived from the C–OH bond, while the peak at 286.1 eV corresponds to C–O/C–N bonds [39,40]. For N 1s, three peaks were observed, including 399.5 eV (N–H on pyrrole nitrogen) [41], 400.7 eV (C–N–C), and 401.5 eV (N-(C)3), respectively (Fig. 2h), which are likely related to the molecular structure of NETH. The O 1s binding energy peaks were found at 531.3 eV, 532.5 eV, and 534.5 eV, which can be attributed to CO, C–O–C and C–O–H groups [40,42], respectively (Fig. 2i). Taken together, all these characterizations were consistent with the previous work, indicating the successful preparation of NETH-CDs.

The production repeatability and storage stability are crucial for the intended applications. For evaluating the production repeatability, the synthesis of NETH-CDs was conducted repeatedly. As shown in Fig. S1a, the excitation and emission spectra from these NETH-CDs did not show dramatic difference, indicating the high reproducibility of NETH-CDs production by electrolysis. To investigate the storage stability of NETH-CDs, the fluorescence emission spectra of as-prepared NETH-CDs solution were measured once a day for seven consecutive days. It was seen that the variation of emission intensity could be ignored (Fig. S1b), suggesting the favorable storage stability of NETH-CDs.

It was seen that the introduction of Sa, HCl and nitrite caused dramatic colorimetric change of NETH-CDs solution, accompanying with the increased absorbance around 538 nm with 5 min reaction window (Fig. 3 a). However, there was no visible UV–Vis absorption profile change if only nitrite was introduced into NETH-CDs solution. The addition of Sa or HCl induced slight and close fluorescence suppression. It is well known that benzenesulfonic acid possesses strong acidity (pKa ∼0.7) similar to sulfuric acid. Thus, the addition of Sa or HCl may cause the protonation of surface groups (i.e., amine and carboxyl), which alters the emission pathway and regulates the fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, the absorption profile of NETH-CDs solution also showed comparable variations during the introduction of Sa and HCl. To figure out the generation of strong absorbance around 538 nm, the UV–Vis absorption spectra of Sa, HCl, nitrite, NETH, and a mixture of them were recorded. As shown in Fig. 3a, visible absorption peak around 538 nm was observed in the mixture solution, and there was no similar absorption profile in sole case. These results suggest that the absorption peak around 538 nm can be assigned for the generated azo dye. Therefore, the strong absorbance located 538 nm in NETH-CDs originated from the formation of azo dye via Griess reaction. In addition, the diazo reaction between nitrite and other chemicals is an important characteristic, which has been well employed for developing new nitrite assays [43–45].

Meanwhile, the introduction of nitrite caused distinct fluorescence quenching in the co-existence of Sa and HCl (Fig. 3b). While the addition of Sa, HCl or nitrite only didn't induce remarkable fluorescence suppression. According to the fluorescence emission profile of NETH-CDs and the UV–Vis absorption profile of formed azo dye, partial overlap could be observed, indicating the possible occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. In addition, the addition of nitrite didn't change the emission profile but supperssed the fluorescence intensity, further suggesting the occurrence of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. In view of the different optical characters, NETH-CDs act as the donor, while azo dye acts as the acceptor. After exposure to nitrite, the fluorescence lifetime of NETH-CDs decreased from 11.01 ns to 4.17 ns (Table S1), with a reduction of 75.7 %. Based on the fluorescence variation, K sv = 0.0053 × 10−6 mol L−1. According to equation (2) K Q=K sv/τ 0, K Q is calculated as 2.07 × 104 L mol−1 s−1, that is lower than the upper limit of dynamic quenching constant 1.0 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1. Increasing temperatures could slightly enhance the quenching effect (Fig. S2), further confirming the dynamic quenching mechanism [46]. However, the emission profile did not show dramatic variation upon the formation of azo dye, ruling out the inner filter effect induced fluorescence quenching [47]. Therefore, multiple quenching mechanisms were involved, including a dynamic quenching process and fluorescence resonance energy transfer [48,49].

Notice that the nanointerfacial Griess reaction is almost instantaneous, it provides great potential for point-of-care testing application [50]. To further enhance the sensing performances, few parameters were optimized, including Sa concentration, HCl concentration, and reaction time. In all cases, the nitrite concentrations were set to be 60 μM. The corresponding absorbance and fluorescence variations were recorded. The Sa concentration was investigated from 0.45 to 5.4 mM (Fig. S3). As shown in Fig. 4 a, the absorbance increment (ΔA) at 538 nm increased apparently when increasing Sa concentration from 0.45 to 1.8 mM and reached a platform with 1.8 mM Sa. The relative fluorescence decrement ((F0–F)/F0) variation trend at 431 nm was similar to the absorbance (Fig. 4b). The plateau under high concentration was probably due to the solubility of SA. High Sa concentration over 1.8 mM could lead to the production of precipitate, then limit the subsequent reaction. Thus, 1.8 mM Sa was chosen for all following assays. It is well known that the Griess assay should be conducted under acid conditions [32]. Thus, HCl dosage would greatly affect the reaction efficiency. As shown in Fig. S4a and Fig. 4c, the absorbance variation decreased with the increasing HCl dosage in the range of 47.7–166.7 mM. A possible reason is that the strongly acidic environment leads to protonation of reactants and restrict their approaching via electrostatic repulsion. In contrast, the fluorescence inhibition increased with the increasing HCl dosage (Fig. S4b and Fig. 4d). The opposite variation trend might be attributed the weak emission of NETH-CDs under strongly acidic conditions. Therefore, the moderate HCl concentration of 95.3 mM was selected for subsequent assays. The reaction time is generally an important factor for achieving rapid and on-site detection. According to the time-dependent signal variations (Fig. S5), we found the nanointerfacial Griess reaction could be finished within 1 min (Fig. 4e and f), resulting in the both colorimetric and fluorometric sensing modes. To ensure the complete reaction, 1 min reaction window was chosen.

In conventional Griess assay, the time-dependent absorbance of NETH for sensing nitrite was also investigated as control. The absorbance gradually increased and reached the maximum at 15 min (Fig. S6). On the basis of our previous work [32], the mechanism for the ultrafast nanointerfacial Griess reaction is ascribed to few aspects. 1. The negatively charged NETH-CDs is closer to positively chareged Sa diazonium salt. 2. The high localized NETH concentration on NETH-CDs benifits the cross-linking reaction to Sa diazonium salt. 3. The active nanointerface is attractive and shows good surface adsorption character. The synergistic interaction of these factors strongly enhances the molecular collision probability, and thus accelerates the Griess reaction kinetics on nanointerface. Interestingly, the absorbance and fluorescence signal were stable even after 2 h (Figs. 4e and 3f), enabling the accurate detection events.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of dual-mode nitrite sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The rapid and dual-mode sensing features make NETH-CDs promising nanoprobes for nitrite detection. To investigate the sensitivities, both UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of NETH-CDs solutions were obtained by introducing nitrite in the concentration range of 0–300 μM. The Sa concentration and HCl concentration were 1.8 mM and 95.3 mM, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5 a, upon the addition of nitrite, the absorbance of NETH-CDs solution gradually increased around 450–600 nm range. The maximum peak wavelength was located around 538 nm, which is assigned for the generation of azo dye [51]. Meanwhile, the solution color underwent variations from light yellow, light orange, pink red, and dark red (>200 μM). In conventional Griess assay, the solution color only changed from colorless to pink red. The deep color indicates the more complete reaction on nanointerface. Using absorbance increment (ΔA) at 538 nm as the signal, it displayed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.998) versus the nitrite concentration in the range of 0.2–100 μM (Fig. 5b). Through linear regression, it could be described as following equation: ΔA = 0.0088 + 0.0097Cnitrite, where Cnitrite is the nitrite concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) for colorimetric sensing mode was calculated to be 0.10 μM (S/N = 3).

In fluorescence mode, the fluorescence signal of NETH-CDs gradually decreased with the increasing nitrite concentration, and was completely eliminated with 300 μM nitrite. Under the UV light illumination, the blue emission of NETH-CDs slowly weakened with the increasing nitrite concentration. Using relative fluorescence decrement ((F0–F)/F0) at 431 nm as the signal, it displayed a good linear relationship (R2 = 0.996) versus the nitrite concentration in the range of 0.2–100 μM (Fig. 5b). Through linear regression, it could be described as following equation: (F0–F)/F0 = 0.0266 + 0.0053Cnitrite, where Cnitrite is the nitrite concentration. The LOD for fluorometric sensing mode was calculated to be 0.08 μM (S/N = 3). The LODs for ion chromatography and spectrophotometry in China National Food Safety Standard (GB 5009.33–2016) were 0.2 mg/kg (∼1.61 μM) and 1.2 mg/kg (∼19.35 μM), respectively. The LODs of proposed NETH-CDs nanoprobes were much lower than that from GB 5009.33–2016. In addition, they were also better than most newly developed methods based on spectrophotometer, colorimetry, fluorescence and electrochemistry [52]. As shown in Table 1 , the sensing performances of our method and other reported works were summarized. In addition to the LODs, the rapid reaction kinetics in this work is also a beneficial feature for fast nitrite sensing. To verify the stability, fifteen repeated measurements of 60 μM nitrite induced optical variations were recorded. It was seen that both absorbance and fluorescence signals didn't show distinct difference between individual measurement, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) values were determined to be 1.0 % and 1.1 % in colorimetry and fluorescence modes (Fig. S7). The low RSD value suggests the high reproducibility of the proposed dual-mode nitrite sensing platform. In a word, the rapid reaction kinetics, dual-mode perception and high reproducibility endow NETH-CDs promising nanoprobes for nitrite detection.

For nitrite sensing in complicated environments, the high specificity over other anions is important. In this case, a few anions were selected for the understanding of nitrite recognition selectivity, including SO4 2−, CO3 2−, CH3OO−, S2−, Cl−, PO4 3−, I−, NO3 −, and Br− (Fig. S8). As shown in Fig. 6 , none of them could cause a similar variation toward absorbance or fluorescence, indicating the high selectivity of nitrite sensing with NETH-CD nanoprobes. It was seen that NO3 − and Br− induced a slight reduction of fluorescence, mainly due to the loose chelation between ions and functional groups [64]. Both colorimetric and fluorometric sensing mode exhibited high selectivity toward nitrite, which is attributed to the specific chemical reaction (Griess reaction). It can be concluded that the high sensitivity and selectivity allow the feasibility of rapid and dual-mode nitrite sensing in complicated environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrite analysis in biological and food samples
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the high sensitivity, selectivity, fast response, the assays were applied to detect nitrite content in biological samples (urine and serum) and food samples (pickled vegetable and cured meat). As shown in Table 2 , the nitrite content was not detected in both urine and serum samples. In addition, spiked samples were also obtained by extra addition of nitrite. With NETH-CDs nanoprobes, the recoveries were found to be 100.5–102.6 %, and the RSD values were less than 4.6 %, indicating the high accuracy of nitrite analysis in biological samples. The nitrite contents in pickled vegetable and cured meat were found to be 11.57 mg/kg and 7.81 mg/kg, respectively. For the extra addition tests, the recoveries were determined to be 101.4–106.0 %, and the RSD values were less than 3.6 %, indicating the capability of nitrite analysis in food samples with NETH-CDs nanoprobes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NETH-CDs-based test strips for visual nitrite detection
ce:section.ce:para: The ultrafast reaction kinetics implies NETH-CDs might be capable of point-of-care testing applications [30,31]. To realize the visual nitrite detection, the test strips were obtained by immersing filtering paper in a NETH-CDs solution and then drying at 45 °C for 2 h. Filtering paper is an inexpensive substrate, and the low-cost process of immobilizing CDs makes this approach economically viable for large-scale production [65]. Additionally, paper-based sensors are lightweight, flexible, and portable, making them ideal platform for on-site and real-time monitoring applications [59]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the test strip initially appears slightly yellow. With increasing nitrite concentration, the test strip turned into red under natural light. Meanwhile, the fluorescence is significantly reduced under the exposure of UV light. To further simply the visual detection, the photographs of all test strips were recorded with a mobile phone under room light and UV light illumination. Through RGB data extraction, the images were analyzed with red channel data (room light illumination) and blue channel data (UV light illumination). The consistent variations trends in comparison to spectrophotometers were obtained.

To verify the on-site detection capability of NETH-CDs-based test strips, nitrite detection in both sewage and lake water samples were tested. As shown in Fig. S9 and 60 μM nitrite induced response could be readily visualized. In addition, the nitrite recovery tests in sewage and lake water samples were conducted. As displayed in Table S2, the recoveries ranged from 101.4 % to 103.3 %, and the RSD values were smaller than 3.5, indicating the relatively high accuracy of on-site nitrite sensing with NETH-CDs-based test strips. Although the results might not that accurate as from UV–Vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy, the easy use and simple operation enhance the potential applications of the test strips.

In summary, we have reported a carbonization-enabled colorimetric and fluorometric dual-mode nitrite sensing system using NETH-CDs nanoprobe, and the nitrite induced both absorbance and fluorescence responses are finished within 1 min. The mechanism study discloses that the ultrafast nanointerfacial Griess assay is attributed to the synergistic interaction of carbonization-engineered electrostatic attraction, enriched localized NETH concentration and surface adsorption capability, which strongly enhances the molecular collision probability and reaction kinetics. The practical application of proposed NETH-CDs nanoprobes is further verified by the accurate nitrite analysis in urine and serum samples. Furthermore, with the association of mobile phone, on-site nitrite detection is also achieved based on NETH-CDs loaded test strips. This work not only reports a NETH-CDs nanoprobe-based dual-mode nitrite sensing platform, but also provides deep understanding of the relationship between chemical reaction kinetics and interfacial interaction on nanosurface. We believe our findings would benefit the exploration of rapid and sensitive detection systems toward various targets by regulating interfacial chemistry.

Jingbo Sun: Writing – original draft, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Tiantian Long: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Hongmei Luo: Validation, Software, Resources. Jiafeng Cao: Software, Resources. Dong Xu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Zhiqin Yuan: Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82160153 and 22074005), the Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Safety Monitoring, Early Warning (2020KFJJ02), Scientific Research Project of Hunan Provincial Department of Education (21A173 and 21B0254), and the Natural Science Foundation of Beijing Municipality (2202038).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of nitrite and nitrate in biological fluids by assays based on the Griess reaction: appraisal of the Griess reaction in the L-arginine/nitric oxide area of research
